questions.stem: Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A patient with new chest pressure and nausea
A patient asking for a pillow
A patient wanting discharge paperwork
A patient asking for ice water

questions.rationale: New chest pressure with nausea may indicate acute cardiac ischemia and must be assessed immediately. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Comfort requests are not the top priority. - C Discharge teaching can wait. - D Water is not urgent compared with possible cardiac symptoms.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "A patient asking for a pillow" moves care toward routine care before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "New chest pressure with nausea may indicate acute cardiac ischemia and must be assessed immediately" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "A patient asking for ice water" moves care toward ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching before circulation and perfusion support.

questions.takeaway: Chest symptoms with nausea are high-priority until proven otherwise.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which action best helps reduce transmission when caring for a patient with a contagious infection?

options.text: Perform appropriate hand hygiene before and after care
Reuse gloves if they still look clean
Skip precautions if the patient feels better
Share equipment without cleaning it

questions.rationale: Hand hygiene remains one of the most effective infection prevention measures.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "Simple infection-control steps still matter most" suggests infection progression, so "Reuse gloves if they still look clean" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.c: "Skip precautions if the patient feels better" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Simple infection-control steps still matter most" points to infection progression. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.d: "Share equipment without cleaning it" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "Simple infection-control steps still matter most" raises concern for infection progression, so infection or sepsis escalation is needed before diagnostic reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Simple infection-control steps still matter most.

visualRationale.title: infection_prevention
visualRationale.caption: Simple infection-control steps still matter most.
visualRationale.conclusion: Simple infection-control steps still matter most.

questions.stem: Which patient should the nurse see first?

options.text: A patient with new chest pressure and diaphoresis
A patient needing help setting up a meal tray
A patient asking when rounds will begin
A patient who wants to know if family can visit later

questions.rationale: Chest pressure with diaphoresis can signal an acute cardiac emergency and must be assessed immediately. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Meal setup is not urgent. - C Rounds timing is nonurgent. - D Visiting questions can wait.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "Chest pressure with diaphoresis can signal an acute cardiac emergency and must be assessed immediately" suggests circulation or perfusion, so "A patient needing help setting up a meal tray" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: "A patient asking when rounds will begin" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Chest pressure with diaphoresis can signal an acute cardiac emergency and must be assessed immediately" points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "A patient who wants to know if family can visit later" moves care toward routine observation before circulation and perfusion support.

questions.takeaway: Acute chest symptoms outrank routine requests.

questions.stem: Which task is best for the nurse to delegate to AP?

options.text: Transporting a stable patient to radiology
Teaching a patient how to use an inhaler
Evaluating a new complaint of dizziness
Deciding whether chest pain needs an ECG

questions.rationale: Transporting a stable patient is a routine, predictable task that can be delegated when the nurse remains available for assessment follow-up. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Teaching requires nursing knowledge. - C Dizziness requires assessment first. - D Chest pain decisions require nursing judgment and escalation.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "- C Dizziness requires assessment first" suggests circulation or perfusion, so "Teaching a patient how to use an inhaler" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: "Evaluating a new complaint of dizziness" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Transporting a stable patient is a routine, predictable task that can be delegated when the nurse remains available" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Deciding whether chest pain needs an ECG" moves care toward routine care before circulation and perfusion support.

questions.takeaway: Delegate routine tasks for stable patients, not assessment or teaching.

questions.stem: Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A patient with chest pain and diaphoresis
A patient asking for a blanket
A patient waiting for discharge teaching tomorrow
A patient requesting help finding a charger

questions.rationale: Chest pain with diaphoresis suggests a potentially unstable cardiovascular event and takes priority over routine comfort or nonurgent requests.

distractorRationales.b: "A patient asking for a blanket" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Chest pain with diaphoresis suggests a potentially unstable cardiovascular event and takes priority over routine comfort or nonurgent" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "A patient with chest pain and diaphoresis" fits the safer response to the cue "Unstable symptoms always come before comfort tasks".
distractorRationales.d: "A patient requesting help finding a charger" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Chest pain with diaphoresis suggests a potentially unstable cardiovascular event and takes priority over routine comfort or nonurgent" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Unstable symptoms always come before comfort tasks.

questions.stem: A patient suddenly becomes confused and pulls at lines. What should the nurse do first?

options.text: Assess the patient for a possible acute cause and protect safety
Assume the confusion is normal in the evening
Leave the patient alone to avoid upsetting them
Focus only on the charting

questions.rationale: Sudden confusion can signal an acute change. The nurse should protect the patient and look for the cause. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Sudden confusion should not be dismissed. - C Leaving the patient alone can increase harm. - D Charting does not come before assessment and safety.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "A patient suddenly becomes confused and pulls at lines" suggests neurologic deterioration, so "Assume the confusion is normal in the evening" would not correct the active problem. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.
distractorRationales.c: "Leave the patient alone to avoid upsetting them" misses the bedside risk. The cue "A patient suddenly becomes confused and pulls at lines" points to neurologic deterioration. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "- D Charting does not come before assessment and safety" suggests neurologic deterioration, so "Focus only on the charting" would not correct the active problem. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.

questions.takeaway: New confusion should be treated as a change in condition, not routine behavior.

questions.stem: A patient becomes much less responsive after previously answering clearly. What is the nurse's first priority?

options.text: Reassess airway, breathing, circulation, and neurologic status
Wait for the patient to wake up naturally
Document that the patient is probably just tired
Offer the patient food before assessing further

questions.rationale: Sudden decreased responsiveness can reflect neurologic, respiratory, circulatory, or metabolic deterioration. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Delay may miss a serious change. - C Tiredness should not be assumed. - D Food does not replace an urgent assessment.

distractorRationales.b: "Wait for the patient to wake up naturally" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Sudden decreased responsiveness can reflect neurologic, respiratory, circulatory, or metabolic deterioration" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.c: "Document that the patient is probably just tired" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Sudden decreased responsiveness can reflect neurologic, respiratory, circulatory, or metabolic deterioration" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Reassess airway, breathing, circulation, and neurologic status" fits the safer response to the cue "Sudden decreased responsiveness is an emergency until proven otherwise".

questions.takeaway: Sudden decreased responsiveness is an emergency until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: A patient suddenly develops slurred speech and one-sided facial droop. What is the nurse's priority?

options.text: Activate the stroke response process immediately
Recheck the patient after the next medication pass
Ask the patient to rest and avoid talking
Document the change and continue routine care

questions.rationale: Sudden focal neurologic changes can signal stroke. Rapid response matters because time-sensitive treatment may be available. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Delay can cost critical treatment time. - C Rest does not address the neurologic emergency. - D Routine care is not appropriate when stroke is possible.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Activate the stroke response process immediately" fits the safer response to the cue "Sudden slurred speech and facial droop are stroke warning signs".
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Activate the stroke response process immediately" fits the safer response to the cue "Sudden slurred speech and facial droop are stroke warning signs".
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Activate the stroke response process immediately" fits the safer response to the cue "Sudden slurred speech and facial droop are stroke warning signs".

questions.takeaway: Sudden slurred speech and facial droop are stroke warning signs.

questions.stem: A pregnant patient at 31 weeks presents with epigastric/RUQ pain, headache, nausea, and malaise. Labs reveal: AST 280 U/L, LDH 950 U/L, platelets 74,000/mm3, hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL with schistocytes on smear, and normal creatinine. Which condition do these findings indicate?

options.text: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)
HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets)
Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)
Viral hepatitis with thrombocytopenia

questions.rationale: HELLP syndrome is a variant of severe preeclampsia: Hemolysis (schistocytes, elevated LDH, low Hgb), Elevated Liver enzymes (AST >70 U/L), Low Platelets (<100,000). RUQ/epigastric pain from hepatic capsule distension is classic. Definitive treatment is delivery. AFLP (A) presents with marked coagulopathy (elevated PT/INR, hypoglycemia, jaundice) due to hepatocyte fat infiltration — different mechanism. TTP (C) presents with the pentad including fever and renal failure. Viral hepatitis (D) would not present with schistocytes and would have different epidemiology.

distractorRationales.a: "Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)" misses the bedside risk. The cue "AFLP (A) presents with marked coagulopathy (elevated PT/INR, hypoglycemia, jaundice) due to hepatocyte fat infiltration — different mechanism" points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.c: "Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "A pregnant patient at 31 weeks presents with epigastric/RUQ pain, headache, nausea" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so bleeding-risk intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; "Viral hepatitis with thrombocytopenia" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before bleeding-risk intervention.

questions.takeaway: HELLP = Hemolysis (schistocytes + elevated LDH) + Elevated Liver enzymes + Low Platelets; treatment is delivery regardless of gestational age.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old patient with bipolar disorder refuses psychiatric hospitalization. He has not slept in 5 days, believes he is receiving messages from aliens, and stated he plans to 'make his neighbor pay' for 'spying on him.' Which criterion most legally supports involuntary psychiatric commitment?

options.text: Presence of a psychiatric diagnosis requiring treatment
Refusal of recommended medication
Danger to others based on specific expressed threat
Inability to care for personal hygiene and nutrition

questions.rationale: Involuntary psychiatric commitment (civil commitment) requires that the person poses a danger to self, danger to others, or is gravely disabled (unable to meet basic needs of food, clothing, shelter due to mental illness). This patient's specific threat to 'make his neighbor pay' combined with active psychosis meets the danger-to-others criterion. Having a psychiatric diagnosis alone (A) is not sufficient for commitment. Medication refusal (B) alone does not meet criteria. While grave disability (D) could apply, the most legally defensible criterion here is the specific threat to another person.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of medication-safety intervention. The stem cue "A 35-year-old patient with bipolar disorder refuses psychiatric hospitalization" makes medication toxicity the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.b: "Refusal of recommended medication" misses the bedside risk. The cue "A 35-year-old patient with bipolar disorder refuses psychiatric hospitalization" points to medication toxicity. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of medication-safety intervention. The stem cue "Medication refusal (B) alone does not meet criteria" makes medication toxicity the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Involuntary commitment requires: danger to self, danger to others (specific threat + mental illness), or grave disability — psychiatric diagnosis alone is insufficient.

questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: "This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision" raises concern for clinical safety risk, so bedside safety action is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma" makes clinical safety risk the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave clinical safety risk untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation" fits the safer response to the cue "Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma".

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: "Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight" moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. The cue "The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching.
distractorRationales.c: The cue "Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting" suggests circulation or perfusion, so "Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it uses teaching before the physiologic risk is controlled. "Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids" fits the safer response to the cue "sodium 128 mEq/L".

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.
